items.q: In attribution theory, is 'luck' an internal or external factor?
items.correct: External - luck lies outside the performer's control.
items.random_wrong: Luck is the same thing as ability.
items.why: Locus of causality places luck outside the self, unlike internal factors such as effort or ability.

items.correct: Crediting success to oneself (internal) but blaming failure on external factors.
items.why: The bias protects self-esteem by taking credit for wins and deflecting blame for losses.

items.q: What can cause learned helplessness in sport?
items.correct: Repeatedly attributing failure to stable, internal causes such as a lack of ability.
items.why: Believing failure is due to fixed personal shortcomings makes performers give up trying.
The answer examiners want is: Stability and controllability. “Locus only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Self-serving bias is the tendency to attribute:
items.why: Correct answer: Success internally, failure externally. “Success externally, failure internally” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If 80% of athletes blame loss on bad luck and 70% credit wins to skill, total athletes showing self-serving pattern (%)?
items.why: Exact answer: 150. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
